does: This paper presents a grounded theory of LLM red teaming through qualitative research, interviewing dozens of practitioners across diverse backgrounds. The work defines red teaming as a limit-seeking, non-malicious manual activity requiring teamwork and an alchemist mindset. The authors characterize the motivations driving practitioners, finding intrinsic motivation rooted in curiosity, fun, and concerns about potential harms from deploying LLMs. Critically, they develop a comprehensive taxonomy organizing the adversarial attack landscape, identifying 12 distinct strategies and 35 different techniques people employ when attempting to cause LLMs to fail or behave abnormally. This structured categorization transforms anecdotal attack knowledge into a systematic framework that captures how practitioners systematically probe model vulnerabilities.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of adversarial robustness and model safety testing, extending beyond earlier work on adversarial examples in vision and NLP to the new domain of large language models. It responds to growing concerns about harmful outputs and misuse of increasingly capable LLMs, connecting to parallel efforts in AI safety, alignment research, and empirical studies of model vulnerabilities. The grounded theory methodology draws on established qualitative research traditions while applying them to this novel human activity. It complements technical red teaming papers and adversarial attack research by centering the human practitioners and their actual strategies, building understanding of how real-world attack discovery happens rather than just documenting attack success.
enables: This taxonomy and characterization of red teaming strategies enables more systematic and efficient vulnerability discovery in LLMs, providing a shared language for practitioners and researchers. The documented techniques can guide both attack and defense efforts, helping safety teams prioritize which failure modes matter most. Understanding red teaming motivations supports better institutional structures for responsible disclosure and collaborative safety work. The framework enables researchers to design targeted robustness evaluations and develop defenses against specific attack classes. Future work can build on this taxonomy to create tools, benchmarks, or training methods that leverage these strategies more systematically, or to study which techniques prove most effective at scale, ultimately improving both model safety practices and coordination between academic and industry red teaming efforts.
